title: Mechanisms of microbial attachment, invasion, persistence, and toxin-mediated damage
body_html:
Microbial Mechanisms of Attachment
Microorganisms employ distinct surface molecules and structures to adhere to host cells, initiating infection.
OrganismAttachment methodClinical relevance
Influenza virusHaemagglutinin binds to sialic acid on respiratory epitheliumInitiates viral entry into airway epithelial cells, leading to influenza
Giardia lambliaSucking disc attaches to intestinal mucosaEnables colonisation of the duodenum and malabsorption syndromes
Plasmodium falciparumInduces expression of PfEMP1 on infected RBCsPfEMP1 binds endothelial receptors, causing sequestration and cerebral malaria
HIVgp120 binds strongly to CD4 antigen on T-helper cellsAllows viral entry and destruction of CD4+ lymphocytes, impairing immunity
Neisseria gonorrhoeaeFimbriae/pili bind to mucosal surfacesPromotes colonisation of the genital tract and immune evasion

Mechanisms of invasion and immune evasion
To persist in the host, pathogens must evade innate and adaptive defences using highly evolved molecular tools.
OrganismInvasion or evasion strategyClinical relevance
Neisseria meningitidisSecretes IgA proteaseDisrupts mucosal immunity, facilitating entry across nasopharyngeal epithelium
Staphylococcus aureusExpresses protein A that binds Fc region of IgGInhibits opsonisation and complement activation
Streptococcus pneumoniaePossesses polysaccharide capsulePrevents phagocytosis by neutrophils, enhancing survival in the bloodstream
Vibrio choleraeFlagellated and highly motilePenetrates intestinal mucus barrier, aiding colonisation and toxin delivery
Mycobacterium tuberculosisInhibits phagosome–lysosome fusion in macrophagesEnables long-term intracellular persistence

Mechanisms of host damage via toxins
Some pathogens produce potent exotoxins that disrupt host cell function, leading to major clinical syndromes:
OrganismToxinMechanism of damageClinical effect
Vibrio choleraeCholera toxinActivates adenylate cyclase in enterocytes → ↑cAMP → ion & water lossProfuse watery diarrhoea
Clostridium tetaniTetanospasminBlocks inhibitory neurotransmitter release (GABA, glycine)Muscle rigidity, spasms (tetanus)
Corynebacterium diphtheriaeDiphtheria toxinInhibits EF-2 → halts protein synthesisCell death, pseudomembrane formation in pharynx
Escherichia coli (ETEC)Heat-labile and heat-stable enterotoxinsStimulate secretion via cAMP/cGMP pathwaysTraveller’s diarrhoea
Bacillus anthracisLethal factorCleaves MAPK pathway kinasesCell death, systemic inflammation (anthrax)
4
0
